question: WHY SHOULD I NEVER PUSH AWAY TO THE OUTSIDE?
content:
Pushing the ball away to the outside fundamentally disrupts the biomechanics of an efficient bowling swing. The pushaway sets the initial trajectory of the entire pendulum; if that first movement is misdirected, the swing is forced to compensate later.
When the ball is pushed away to the outside, it almost inevitably travels behind the body at the top of the backswing. This places the swing off the intended swing plane and creates several technical problems:
1. Compromised swing path and accuracy
A ball that moves behind the body must return forward on a curved path. This rounded swing shape reduces repeatability and makes it much harder to consistently send the ball through the same launch angle and target line. Accuracy suffers because the swing is no longer moving on a straight, gravity driven plane toward the target.
2. Forced corrections and muscle involvement
Once the ball is behind the body, the bowler is forced to “save” the shot on the downswing. This usually involves pulling the arm, rotating the shoulder, or steering the ball back toward the line. These compensations replace gravity with muscle, making timing less reliable and increasing physical tension.
3. Excessive or uncontrolled rotation
An outside pushaway encourages the swing to move around the body rather than under it. As a result, the hand often comes around the side of the ball too early, creating excessive or unstable axis rotation. This is why the motion begins to resemble a golf style swing rather than a vertical bowling pendulum.
4. Reduced consistency under pressure
Because a sideways pushaway relies on compensations, it becomes especially unreliable when timing, tempo, or confidence changes, such as during competition. What may “work” occasionally in practice tends to break down under pressure.
The correct principle
A proper pushaway must be aligned with the intended target line (E.T.T, Everything Towards the Target). When the ball is pushed straight forward (or very slightly inside the target line for modern styles), gravity naturally keeps the swing on plane. The ball stays outside the body at the top of the swing, returns close to the ankle, and allows a clean, repeatable release with the hand under the ball.
In simple terms:
The pushaway programs the swing.
If it starts in the wrong direction, the entire motion must compensate.
A straight, aligned pushaway produces a free swing, better timing, cleaner rotation, and far greater accuracy.
